facts.text: Roman history stretches from the city's legendary founding through the Republic, the Empire, and the Byzantine continuation in the East.
According to tradition, Rome was founded in 753 BC by Romulus.
The Roman Republic developed systems of elected offices, a Senate, and written law that influenced later governments.
The Punic Wars against Carthage helped Rome become the dominant power in the western Mediterranean.
Julius Caesar crossed the Rubicon in 49 BC, triggering a civil war that transformed Roman politics.
Caesar was assassinated on the Ides of March in 44 BC.
Augustus became Rome's first emperor and began the Pax Romana, a long stretch of relative stability and expansion.
The Colosseum opened in AD 80 and became one of the empire's most famous amphitheaters.
Roman roads, aqueducts, concrete, and legal systems became some of the empire's most lasting achievements.
Latin, the language of Rome, strongly shaped Romance languages such as Italian, Spanish, French, Portuguese, and Romanian.
The Roman Empire reached its greatest territorial extent under Emperor Trajan in the early 2nd century AD.
Constantine embraced Christianity and founded Constantinople, reshaping the empire's religious and political future.
The western Roman Empire is traditionally said to have fallen in AD 476, though Roman rule continued in the East for centuries.
Roman history still influences politics, architecture, military strategy, law, language, and popular culture today.
The Roman Republic shaped ideas of citizenship, senate rule, and imperial expansion before the rise of the emperors.
Its political conflicts and institutions remain central to the study of Roman history.
Roman Republic is one of the most important topics associated with Roman History.
Books, documentaries, and classrooms that cover Roman History frequently highlight Roman Republic.
Learning about Roman Republic helps explain a major part of Roman History.
For many readers, Roman Republic stands out as one of the defining subjects in Roman History.
The story of Roman Republic is closely tied to the broader themes of Roman History.
Roman Republic is often used to introduce students to the history and significance of Roman History.
Many surveys of Roman History return again and again to the importance of Roman Republic.
From public memory to scholarship, Roman Republic plays a notable role in how people understand Roman History.
One reason Roman History remains so compelling is the lasting significance of Roman Republic.
The legacy of Roman Republic continues to shape how Roman History is discussed today.
